coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots for the selective detection of chlortetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Lee, Yu-Sheng
Hu, Cho-Chun
Chiu, Tai-Chia
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been used for the fluorescent turn-on detection of chlortetracycline (CTC) with high selectivity and sensitivity. In this study, CDs were fabricated via electrochemical treatment using diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid and ethylenediamine as precursors. The synthesized CDs exhibited blue fluorescence under ultraviolet irradiation and a maximum emission of 407 nm at an excitation wavelength of 325 nm; the calculated quantum yield was 2.5%. Moreover, the CDs demonstrated excellent water solubility and photobleaching resistance as well as good stability. In the presence of CTC, a considerable enhancement in fluorescence emission was observed, based on aggregation-induced emission enhancement owing to the complexation of CTC and CDs, thus providing the foundation for quantifying CTC. Under optimal conditions, the linear range was 0.8–10 μM (R2 = 0.9944), and the corresponding limit of detection was 16.9 nM. The proposed sensing system was successfully applied for determining CTC in actual samples with good recoveries between 91.3% and 110.6%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregation-induced emission enhancement
Chlortetracycline
Electrochemical synthesis
Fluorescent carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and optical properties of carbon dots
Fluorescence stability of the carbon dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan under contract numbers MOST 109-2113-M-143-002 and MOST 110-2113-M-143-001 . The authors are also thankful to the Joint Center of High Valued Instruments, National Sun Yat-sen University, for the TEM (EM001400) measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and size distribution of CDs in the (a, b) absence and (c, d) presence of CTC.
(a) XPS full-survey spectrum (inset shows the element content) and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s of CDs.
UV–vis absorption (black) and fluorescence excitation (blue) and emission (red) spectra of the CDs. Inset: Images of the CD solution under (a) daylight and (b) UV light at 365 nm.
(a) Images of multiple antibiotics (100 μM) with CDs under UV light illumination. (b) Selectivity of CDs toward multiple antibiotics.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs at various CTC concentrations ranging from 0 to 800 μM. (b) Relationship between F/F0 and CTC concentration. The inset shows the linear relationship between F/F0 and CTC concentration ranging from 0.8 to 10 μM.
Schematic of the electrochemical synthesis of the CDs for determining CTC via aggregation-induced emission enhancement caused by the complexation of CTC and CDs.
Tolerance limits of certain potentially interfering species in the determination of 10 μM of CTC.

row.entry: Arginine, Glutamic acid, Glutathione, Glycine, Histidine, Phenylalanine
K+, Na+, Co2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Al3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of CTC in actual samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Electrochemical synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots for the selective detection of chlortetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Science, National Taitung University, Taitung, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Science, National Taitung University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Science, National Taitung University, Taitung, Taiwan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Department of Applied Science, National Taitung University, 369, Section 2, University Road, Taitung 95092, Taiwan.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Science, National Taitung University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been used for the fluorescent turn-on detection of chlortetracycline (CTC) with high selectivity and sensitivity. In this study, CDs were fabricated via electrochemical treatment using diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid and ethylenediamine as precursors. The synthesized CDs exhibited blue fluorescence under ultraviolet irradiation and a maximum emission of 407 nm at an excitation wavelength of 325 nm; the calculated quantum yield was 2.5%. Moreover, the CDs demonstrated excellent water solubility and photobleaching resistance as well as good stability. In the presence of CTC, a considerable enhancement in fluorescence emission was observed, based on aggregation-induced emission enhancement owing to the complexation of CTC and CDs, thus providing the foundation for quantifying CTC. Under optimal conditions, the linear range was 0.8–10 μM (R2 = 0.9944), and the corresponding limit of detection was 16.9 nM. The proposed sensing system was successfully applied for determining CTC in actual samples with good recoveries between 91.3% and 110.6%.

ce:section.ce:para: Certain members of the tetracycline (TC) antibiotic family, including chlortetracycline (CTC), doxycycline (DOX), oxytetracycline (OTC), and TC, are well-known multifunctional antibacterial drugs [1]. Owing to their broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity, low cost, and high quality, these antibiotics have extensively been used in treating infectious diseases caused by pathogenic bacteria for protecting both human and animal health [2]. However, the excessive use of antibiotics in medicine, aquaculture, and animal husbandry has led to their accumulation in the ecosystem. Many considerable side effects to humans, such as hepatotoxicity, anaphylactic reaction, gastrointestinal disorders, and liver damage, are attributed to the ingestion of these environmental antibiotic residues [3,4]. This contamination leads to the inhibition of microbial activity and increases the drug resistance of pathogens [2]. Among TC antibiotics, CTC is considered one of the most harmful veterinary drugs [5]. The high aqueous solubility of CTC may pose a direct threat to organisms and environment. To protect human health and environment, it is extremely desirable to efficiently monitor CTC levels using simple and reliable analytical methods.

Various analytical techniques have been successfully used for measuring TC antibiotics, including high-performance liquid chromatography [6,7], capillary electrophoresis [8,9], immunoassays [10,11], surface-enhanced Raman scattering [4], and electrochemical methods [12,13]. Although most methods have high sensitivity and good accuracy, many inherent limitations limit their application for on-site analysis, including the requirement for sophisticated instruments, time-consuming sample preparation procedures, and skilled technicians. Recently, because of their speed, noninvasive nature, convenience, ease of operation, and high sensitivity, fluorescence-based sensing approaches have been extensively applied for detecting specific target analytes [14,15]. As a related development, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were discovered in 2004. CDs belong to a novel 0D carbonaceous nanomaterial having a size under 10 nm. They have received considerable attention because of their unique optical properties, including ease of preparation, low toxicity, excellent aqueous solubility, good photostability, high biocompatibility, and chemical inertness [16,17]. Various methods, such as laser ablation, arc discharge, combustion, pyrolysis, hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis, electrochemical oxidation, and microwave-assisted synthesis, have been used for preparing CDs [18,19]. Owing to their unique characteristics, CDs have been extensively applied in biosensing [20], biomedical imaging [21], optoelectronic devices [22], photocatalysis [22], energy devices [23], drug delivery [24], and security inks [25].

Moreover, CDs have been used as fluorescent probes for detecting TC antibiotics [26–31]. Qu et al. [27] reported the development of dual emission CDs synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of ascorbic acid and ethylene glycol and their application for detecting TCs using direct and indirect approaches. The indirect approach was used for detecting TCs in milk, fish, and pork meat samples, thus leading to acceptable recoveries. Liu et al. [28] established an assay for determining TCs based on blue–green fluorescent CDs in milk samples, along with satisfying recoveries. In this case, the CDs were fabricated as a portable strip sensor for the visual on-site screening of TCs. Li et al. [29] used red emissive CDs prepared from neutral red and thiourea to sense TCs based on an aggregation-induced emission mechanism. Li et al. [30] solvothermally synthesized CDs using p-phenylenediamine and acetic acid and used them for determining CTC. Recently, Zhang et al. [31] synthesized nitrogen-doped CDs from hawthorn powder and used them to detect CTC in pork samples, thus obtaining good recoveries. To summarize, fluorescent CDs appear to have considerable potential for use as highly selective fluorescent probes for the quantitative determination of CTC.

Based on this aim, a simple one-step approach was herein developed for synthesizing water-soluble CDs through the electrochemical carbonization of diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA) and ethylenediamine (EDA). The synthesized CDs exhibited blue fluorescence at 407 nm, a uniform size distribution, excellent stability, and good water solubility. The spectroscopic results demonstrated that multiple amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl functional groups were distributed on the surface of CDs.

CDs were used as fluorescent probes for the highly selective and sensitive determination of CTC. The sensing mechanism was based on aggregation-induced emission enhancement caused by the complexation of CTC and CDs ( Scheme 1). The fluorescent probes were applied for detecting CTC in actual samples with acceptable recoveries.

DTPA, EDA, phosphoric acid, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, trisodium phosphate, quinine sulfate, creatinine, glucose, urea, amino acids (arginine, glutathione, glutamic acid, glycine, histidine, and phenylalanine), antibiotics (cefaclor, cefadroxil, DOX, lincomycin, OTC, penicillin G, TC, and tylosin), and the other salts used in this study were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (≥ 18.2 MΩ cm−1) was used to prepare all aqueous solutions.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded using a U-2900 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan), and fluorescence spectra were obtained using an RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired using a Nicolet iS5 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) using the KBr pellet method. X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were recorded on a D8 ADVANCE (Bruker AXS, Karlsruhe, Germany) with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) at 40 kV and 40 mA. The elements of CDs were identified using a K-alpha X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). Dynamic light scattering measurements and zeta potentials were obtained using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 particle size analyzer (Malvern, Worcestershire, UK).

The CDs were synthesized using a simple electrochemical method similar to one previously reported [32]. In brief, DTPA (0.3933 g, 1 mmol) was dissolved in a 20-mL bottle containing NaOH (0.1 M, 15 mL), and EDA (201 μL, 3 mmol) was then added. The solution was stirred for 1 min, and a 10-V DC potential was applied for 2 h until the transparent solution had turned yellow. The solution was purified and stored as per procedures published in our previous study [33].

Cefaclor, cefadroxil, CTC, DOX, lincomycin, OTC, penicillin G, TC, and tylosin were evaluated using CDs to confirm their selectivity to these antibiotics. In a 2-mL vial, a final CD concentration of 0.16 mg mL−1 was maintained with an antibiotic concentration of 100 μM. After mixing for 2 h, fluorescence spectra were obtained at room temperature with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The change in relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) was used to evaluate the selectivity of CDs. For this calculation, F0 is the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 407 nm in the absence of antibiotics, and F is the fluorescence intensity in the presence of antibiotics. Anti-interference ability and sensitivity tests (0–800 μM) of the sensing system were performed using the same conditions.

The samples of lake water, artificial urine, and CTC ointment were used to confirm the analytical performance of the system. The lake water sample was collected from Jinsin Lake at the National Taitung University (Taitung, Taiwan), and the CTC ointment was purchased from a local pharmacy. The artificial urine was prepared as per a previously published protocol [34] by dissolving all reagents (Table S1) in distilled water and making the total volume of the solution to 1.000 L. A known amount of CTC was added to three actual samples, followed by pretreatment procedures. The CTC ointment (0.1 g) was weighed and transferred in a 15-mL tube. Then, 10 mL of ultrapure water was added, and the mixture was sonicated for 1 h. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 10 min and filtered using a 0.22-μm-sized nitrocellulose filter. To remove any suspended particles, the lake water sample and artificial urine were subjected to the abovementioned centrifugation and filtration procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the fluorescence properties of CDs, the effects of the ratio of DTPA to EDA on the quantum yield were examined. Multiple CDs were synthesized at various molar ratios of DTPA and EDA (Table S2). As per calculations published in a previous study [33], their relative fluorescence quantum yields were calculated using a quinine sulfate (quantum yield 54%) solution as a reference. Synthesized CDs with the highest quantum yield of 2.5%, which were produced with a DTPA to EDA ratio of 1:3, were selected as fluorescent probes for additional experiments.

The morphology and size distribution of CDs were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1, CDs were quasi-spherical in shape, uniformly dispersed, and between 0.7 and 1.9 nm in diameter. From the diameters of 100 CDs, the average was estimated to be 1.2 nm. The XRD pattern (Fig. S1) demonstrates a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 21.9°, which corresponds to the highly disordered and amorphous nature of CD surfaces [35]. FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. S2) and XPS ( Fig. 2) were performed to explore the structural composition of the surfaces and analyze these elements. The FT-IR spectrum had a broad band in the region of 3000–3500 cm−1, which was attributed to O–H/N–H stretching vibrations [36]. A low-intensity peak at 2955 cm−1 was attributed to C–H stretching vibrations [36]. Peaks at 1653 and 1583 cm−1 were assigned to CO and CC stretching vibrations, respectively [37]. An intense peak at 1402 cm−1 was identified as being attributed to C–N stretching vibration [37], which confirmed the doping of nitrogen. A peak at 1114 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of C–O [36]. The XPS spectrum (Fig. 2) of CDs revealed three characteristic peaks of C1s (285.08 eV), N1s (399.08 eV), and O1s (530.08 eV), indicating the CDs were composed of C, N, and O with atomic percentages of 60.01%, 23.60%, and 16.39%, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2b) was resolved to three peaks at 284.80, 285.15, and 286.80 eV, which were attributed to CC, C–C/C–H/C–N, and CO bonds, respectively [38]. Three nitrogen-related bonding peaks belonging to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and quaternary N bonds were fitted at 398.68, 399.63, and 400.98 eV, respectively, in the high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c). [39]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) was divided in two peaks at 530.48 and 531.08 eV, indicating the existence of CO and CO bonds, respectively [40]. These observations agreed with FT-IR analysis. Based on XPS and FT-IR analysis results, CDs were rich with multiple nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups (such as amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups) on their surfaces, thus resulting in excellent water solubility and stability of the CD solution [41].

To examine the optical properties of CDs, their UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra were obtained. As shown in Fig. 3, the UV–vis absorption spectrum exhibited a wide absorption band with two absorption regions in the ranges of 200–250 and 300–350 nm. These could be attributed to the π-π * electronic transitions of CC bonds and n-π * electronic transitions of CO bonds, respectively [42]. The fluorescence spectra revealed that the maximum emission wavelength was located at 407 nm when the excitation wavelength was 325 nm. The color of the CD solution was light yellow under daylight, but blue fluorescence was emitted upon UV irradiation (Fig. 3, inset). When excited using wavelengths ranging from 260 to 330 nm at increments of 10 nm, the corresponding maximum fluorescence emission peaks remained at 407 nm (Fig. S3). The fluorescence intensity initially increased and then gradually decreased, thus reaching a maximum at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The excitation-independent fluorescence behavior might be related to the CDs’ narrow size distribution and uniform surface states [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence stability of the carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the fluorescence stability of CDs, the effects of pH, ionic strength, and UV irradiation time on fluorescence were assessed. When the pH was increased from 2.0 to 12.0 (Fig. S4a), the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased. The higher fluorescence intensity under low pH conditions (pH 2.0–5.0) may have been attributed to the protonation of carboxyl groups under acidic conditions, which induced the aggregation of CDs, thereby enhancing the fluorescence intensity. The zeta potential measurements (Fig. S4b) of CDs at different pH values demonstrated that CDs were almost neutral at pH values of 2.0 and 3.0. The reduced repulsion force on the surface of CDs should cause their aggregation, enhancing the fluorescence intensity.

The fluorescence intensity of CDs exhibited slight fluctuations as NaCl concentrations increased to 1.0 M (Fig. S4c). Under UV irradiation at 320 nm for 60 min, it remained stable (Fig. S4d). All these results show that CDs possessed excellent fluorescence stability, suggesting that they have considerable potential for use as fluorescent probes.

Because of the presence of multiple functional groups on the surfaces of the CDs, they were tested as fluorescent probes for detecting CTC. The pH and incubation time were optimized to achieve high sensitivity and a wide linear range of CTC detection. As shown in Fig. S5a, the fluorescence intensity rapidly increased when varying pH values from 8.0 to 10.0, whereas the fluorescence intensity remained constant in the pH range of 4.0–7.0. The effect of incubation time on CTC detection was investigated (Fig. S5b). The fluorescence of the sensing system gradually increased, reaching equilibrium at ~120 min. To summarize, a pH of 9.0 and an incubation time of 120 min were selected as optimal conditions.

To explore the sensing mechanism for the highly selective detection of CTC, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence lifetime analysis, TEM, zeta potential analysis, FT-IR spectrometry, and XPS were performed. Fig. S6 shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs with and without CTC. A shift in the absorption peak of the CDs from 346 to 369 nm was observed in the presence of CTC, indicating that π-π interactions occurred between the CTC and CDs, thus forming the complexes. In the presence of CTC, the particle sizes of CDs increased from 1.24 to 3.69 nm (Fig. 1), indicating that CDs were slightly aggregated. When CTC was added to the CDs, a considerable enhancement in the fluorescence emission spectra was observed, which may have been caused by aggregation-induced emission enhancement resulting from the complexation of CTC and CDs [43]. Enhanced fluorescence of CDs was observed in the solid state (Fig. S7). CTC has strong interaction with CDs via hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions, thus inducing the complexation of CTC and CDs and restricting their intramolecular vibrations and rotations. Thus, the primary reason for the enhanced fluorescence emission of CDs observed after the addition of CTC should be associated with a reduction in the non-radiative transition rate and an increase in the lifetime of the excitation state [44]. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs (Fig. S8) was reported to increase with CTC concentration, i.e., 3.6, 3.8, 4.5, and 5.1 ns for CTC concentrations of 0, 1, 10, and 100 μM, respectively.

The pKa values of CTC for tricarbonylamide, phenolic diketone, and dimethylamine moieties have been reported as 3.3 (pKa1), 7.4 (pKa2), and 9.3 (pKa3), respectively [45]. At a pH of 9.0, the enolic groups of phenolic diketone moiety should disassociate as anions, and the CTC should exist in a negative form. Therefore, as shown in Fig. S8, when CTC interacts with CDs, the zeta potential should be more negative.

A comparison of FT-IR spectra (Fig. S10) of the CTC, CDs, and CTC with the CDs demonstrated many differences. After adding CTC to CDs, the band in the region of 3000–3500 cm−1 (O–H/N–H stretching vibration) broadened because of the hydrogen bonding interactions between the CTC and CDs [46]. The absorption peaks at 1653 (CO stretching vibration) and 1583 cm−1 (CC stretching vibration) weakened; however, the peak at 1114 cm−1 (C–O stretching vibration) was enhanced in the presence of CTC. These changes may have resulted from the interactions between the CTC and CDs, and they were similar to the XPS observations (Fig. S11, Table S3).

Comparing the XPS results of CDs in the absence and presence of CTC, the contents of C, N, and O changed from 60.01%, 23.60%, and 16.39% to 64.73%, 17.58%, and 17.55%, respectively, when CTC was added, and few chloride atoms (0.13%) were identified, suggesting that the CTC may have adsorbed on the CDs. Therefore, the sensing mechanism is derived from the hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions between the CTC and CDs that occur during complex formation.

To confirm the selectivity of the CDs for CTC, the fluorescence response of the CDs toward different antibiotics, including cefaclor, cefadroxil, CTC, DOX, lincomycin, OTC, penicillin G, TC, and tylosin, were investigated. Fig. 4 shows that only CTC caused an obvious enhancement in fluorescence while the other antibiotics displayed negligible effects on fluorescence intensity. As CTC, DOX, OTC, and TC belong to the TC family, they have similar chemical structures. The major difference is that CTC contains one Cl atom, which could lead to π-π conjugate interaction with the CDs, thus resulting in the enhancement of interactions between CTC and CDs [30].

The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (Fig. S6) show that only CTC exhibited a shift in wavelength. The zeta potential of CDs decreased from −4.8 to −13.2 mV, further indicating that the surface states of CDs were altered after interaction with CTC (Fig. S8). To summarize, the results demonstrate that CDs exhibited excellent specificity as fluorescent probes for determining CTC.

To evaluate the feasibility of this sensing system for CTC detection in actual samples, the effects of potentially interfering species, including other antibiotics, metal ions, anions, and amino acids, on the detection of 10 μM CTC were investigated. As summarized in Table 1, the CDs exhibited different tolerance levels toward different species. None of these species exhibited interference with CTC detection, indicating that the sensing system can be used to detect CTC with high selectivity.

The fluorescence spectra of CDs were recorded in the presence of various concentrations of CTC under optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 5, the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually increased with increase in CTC concentration (from 0 to 800 μM). In the range of 0.8–10 μM, a good linear relationship was obtained between F/F0 and CTC concentration with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9944. The linear equation was as follows:
(1) F/F0 = 0.6742 × [CTC (μM)] + 0.9602,

where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CDs in the absence and presence of CTC, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 16.9 nM, based on 3σ/s, where σ represents the standard deviation of 10 blank measurements and s is the slope of the calibration curve [47]. The precisions of intraday (n = 5) and interday (n = 5) measurements (1.0 μM), expressed as relative standard deviations (RSD), were 0.54% and 0.87%, respectively, under optimal conditions. Therefore, the sensing system had a wide linear range and high sensitivity. The proposed system had a detection range and LOD comparable to those reported in other studies on CTC detection (Table S4).

To explore the feasibility of practical application of this sensing system, the fluorescent probes were used to detect CTC in actual samples. A known concentration of CTC was spiked in samples of lake water, artificial urine, and CTC ointment, which were pretreated before analysis. Table 2 lists the recoveries and RSDs of various CTC concentrations. The results demonstrated that the CD-based sensing system for the CTC determination led to satisfactory recoveries between 91.3% and 110.6% with RSDs below 2.8%. These results support the argument that CDs are reliable for and capable of determining CTC in actual samples.

In summary, a novel turn-on sensing system based on the usage of CDs as fluorescent probes was developed for detecting CTC. Blue-emissive CDs were synthesized via a simple electrochemical method using DTPA and EDA as precursors. CDs had good water solubility, photobleaching resistance, and stability. In the presence of CTC, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was significantly enhanced because of the aggregation-induced emission enhancement via hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions forming the complexes. The fluorescent probes were demonstrated to be both sensitive and selective for detecting CTC. This sensing system was thus confirmed to have a good linear range of 0.8–10 μM with a low LOD at 16.9 nM. Therefore, the recovery rates of CTC in the real samples were in the range of 91.3–110.6% with RSDs below 2.8% revealing the high reliability and accuracy of the sensing system.

Yu-Sheng Lee: Methodology, Software, Data curation, Visualization, Investigation, Validation. Cho-Chun Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology. Tai-Chia Chiu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft preparation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan under contract numbers MOST 109-2113-M-143-002 and MOST 110-2113-M-143-001. The authors are also thankful to the Joint Center of High Valued Instruments, National Sun Yat-sen University, for the TEM (EM001400) measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2022.107413.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tetracyclines in the environment: an overview on the occurrence, fate, toxicity, detection, removal methods, and sludge management
Sci. Total Environ.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Scaria, K.V. Anupama, P.V. Nidheesh, Tetracyclines in the environment: An overview on the occurrence, fate, toxicity, detection, removal methods, and sludge management, Sci. Total Environ. 771 (2021) 145291. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2021.145291.

Sci. Total Environ.
